does: This paper demonstrates how GPT-4 can be deployed as a content moderator and policy development tool, showing that the model can consistently apply content policies to flag problematic material while simultaneously informing policy refinement through its labeling patterns. The work establishes workflows where GPT-4 makes moderation decisions, tracks these decisions to identify policy gaps or inconsistencies, and uses aggregate patterns to suggest policy improvements. The authors report achieving more consistent label application across diverse content types compared to traditional human-only approaches, while reducing moderator workload and accelerating the feedback loop between policy detection and policy adjustment.
connects: This work builds on the long-standing challenge of scaling content moderation, which has traditionally relied on teams of human moderators facing consistency, scalability, and well-being constraints. It extends prior efforts to use machine learning for classification and filtering by leveraging large language models' improved semantic understanding and instruction-following capabilities. The paper connects to earlier research on using automation to support or partially replace human content moderation, as well as work on consistency in human-in-the-loop systems, while responding to the broader trend of applying large foundation models to complex real-world tasks requiring nuanced judgment and value alignment.
enables: This approach opens pathways for scaling content governance globally while maintaining consistency, since policy development can now iterate faster through automated labeling feedback. Future work could explore using similar methods for developing policies in other regulated domains, and investigating how LLM moderators might be cascaded with human review for high-stakes decisions. The framework potentially enables organizations to test policy variants more efficiently and adapt to emerging harms in near real-time. It also raises questions about relying on a single model's values for moderation, pointing toward research on multi-model ensemble approaches, transparent policy specification, and auditing LLM moderators for bias and gaming risk.
